coredata.dc:title: Gadolinium-doped carbon quantum dots derived from dextran for magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) contrast agent and fluorescence imaging
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ghahremani, Hanieh
Molaei, Mohammad Jafar
Salimi, Esmaeil
coredata.dc:description: Noninvasive imaging methods such as magnetic resonance and fluorescence imaging (FI) have received great attention in the diagnosis of diseases. In this research gadolinium-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized for the application as multimodal magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) contrast agents and FI probes. The CQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of dextran, polyethyleneimine (PEI), and a Gd-containing drug. The CQDs with sizes below 10 nm have fluorescence emission upon excitation with wavelengths in the range of 340–380 nm. The CQDs preserve their photoluminescence (PL) in environments with different ionic strengths (0.005–0.5 M of NaCl) and pH of 4–9. The CQDs incubated with mouse fibroblast cells successfully penetrated the cells and were used in FI. The CQDs were used as magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) contrast agents and showed a longitudinal relaxivity value of 0.272 mM−1s−1. The results confirmed that the Gd-doped CQDs derived from dextran can be used for multimodal imaging in biomedical diagnosis applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence
MRI
Contrast agent
Diagnosis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Gd-doped carbon quantum dots

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The author would like to thank the Shahrood University of Technology, the Iran National Brain Mapping Laboratory, and the Iran Nanotechnology Initiative Council for the financial support of this project.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic illustration that shows the synthesis method of the CQDs from dextran and their application in bioimaging and MRI.
Characterization of the DEX-PEI-Gd-7h CQDs sample (a) XRD pattern, (b) AFM images, (c) size distribution profile of the dots, and (d) zeta potential distribution.

FTIR spectra of the dextran, dextran that is hydrothermally treated (3 h), dextran and PEI solution which is hydrothermally treated for 7 and 14 h, as well as hydrothermally-treated solution of dextran, PEI, and contrast agent.

HRTEM images of the CQDs (DEX-PEI-Gd-7h sample, (a) The crystalline dots with sizes below 10 nm, and (b) lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.24 nm which is consistent with the (1120) lattice facet of graphene.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of the different hydrothermally synthesized samples, (b) the fluorescence emission spectra of the synthesized DEX-PEI-Gd-7h sample by excitation at wavelengths in the range of 340–480 nm, and (c) the photographs of hydrothermally treated samples under UV irradiation.
Comparison between PL emission spectra of the CQDs by excitation wavelengths of 340 nm, 400 nm, 440 nm, and 480 nm.

The effect of pH on the PL spectra of the CQDs in the pH range of 4–9 by excitation at (a) 340 nm and (b) 380 nm. (c) The maximum intensity of PL emission in environments with different pH values by excitation at 340 nm and (d) 380 nm.

The PL spectra of the synthesized CQDs in the environments with different ionic strengths by excitation at (a) 340 nm and (b) 380 nm. (c) The variations of maximum PL intensity of the CQDs in environments with different ionic strength by the excitation at 340 nm and (d) 380 nm.
EDS elemental map of the CQDs solution showing (a) the map for C, O, and Gd alongside (b) individual maps for the elements.
The mouse connective tissue-L929 fibroblast cells that have been incubated with CQDs (a) bright-field images and (b) fluorescence images.
(a) T1-weighted MR images of Gd-doped CQDs with various Gd content. (b) The linear relationship between longitudinal relaxivity (r1) and the concentrations of Gd in the samples.
Comparison of this research with other similar works of CQDs.

row.entry: Stability in environments with different pH
Stability in environments with different ionic strength
Carbonization and acid treatment of walnut shells
Low PL intensity at pH < 3 and pH > 9
PL was reduced by 10 % by the increase in NaCl concentration from 0 to 3 M
Decreased PL in pH 14 and pH 1, but constant PL in pH 2–13
Almost constant PL in the range of 0–4 M NaCl solution
Increasing the PL intensity by increasing pH from 1.5 to 12 by excitation at 390 nm
PL intensity decreases under an acidic pH of 2–4 and an alkaline pH of 10–12
Stable PL in the range of 0–2 M NaCl solution
The PL emission changes from blue to green color with increasing pH from 1 to 13
Fluorescent indicator, pH sensor, and invisible ink
Chromatic response to the pH values in the range of 2.2–12.0
Stable PL in pH values of 4–9
Stable PL in NaCl solution in the range of 0–0.5 M

head.ce:title: Gadolinium-doped carbon quantum dots derived from dextran for magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) contrast agent and fluorescence imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Shahrood University of Technology, Shahrood, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Chemical and Materials Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Shahrood University of Technology, Shahrood, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Noninvasive imaging methods such as magnetic resonance and fluorescence imaging (FI) have received great attention in the diagnosis of diseases. In this research gadolinium-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized for the application as multimodal magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) contrast agents and FI probes. The CQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of dextran, polyethyleneimine (PEI), and a Gd-containing drug. The CQDs with sizes below 10 nm have fluorescence emission upon excitation with wavelengths in the range of 340–380 nm. The CQDs preserve their photoluminescence (PL) in environments with different ionic strengths (0.005–0.5 M of NaCl) and pH of 4–9. The CQDs incubated with mouse fibroblast cells successfully penetrated the cells and were used in FI. The CQDs were used as magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) contrast agents and showed a longitudinal relaxivity value of 0.272 mM−1s−1. The results confirmed that the Gd-doped CQDs derived from dextran can be used for multimodal imaging in biomedical diagnosis applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: CQDs are zero-dimensional carbon-core nanostructures with sizes typically below 10 nm with a quasi-spherical shape. CQDs have excessive carboxyl groups which brings them water-solubility despite the low water solubility of carbon itself [1,2]. CQDs possess unique properties including high chemical stability, fluorescence emission, multicolor optical properties, excellent biocompatibility, water solubility, excellent physicochemical properties, and lower toxicity. Due to their properties, they have found applications in different areas such as drug delivery, photocatalysis, fluorescent sensing, optoelectrical devices, and bioimaging. The CQDs take advantage of quantum confinement and visual stability in their FI. The color of most of the CQDs is blue under 395 nm UV irradiation. The fluorescence properties of CQDs are also affected by the synthesis method and the precursors [3]. The CQDs could be synthesized from various carbon-containing sources including bio-based sources such as microorganisms, polysaccharides, agricultural waste, food waste, and biomolecules [4].

Most of the synthesized CQDs are composed of C, N, and O elements. They can be synthesized from various synthesis methods such as hydrothermal, solvothermal, electrochemical, arc discharge, laser ablation, microwave processes, ultrasonic, etc. Among the synthesis methods of CQDs, the hydrothermal synthesis method is a low-cost, one-step approach, environmentally friendly, and simple method that acts at low temperatures with a self-created pressure [5].

MRI has received great attention due to its high-depth penetration into the tissue and high spatial resolution which provides anatomical details on organs and soft tissues [6]. One of the problems in MRI is low contrast in the images which requires the application of contrast agents for improving the contrast of the images [7]. Gadolinium (Gd) has proved to show high efficiency as a contrast agent due to its excellent magnetic properties. However, gadolinium ions (Gd3+) are toxic in the human body since they cause cardiovascular and neurologic toxicity, induce changes in intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) levels, and inhibit calcium channels. Therefore, for Gd-based MRI contrast agents, it is essential to prevent Gd3+ leakage. Gd-containing nanoparticles and chelates have been developed as excellent probes for MRI [8].

The FI technique has a high sensitivity but has some limitations due to the low penetration of light into tissue. Therefore, FI is more suitable for animal imaging. In contrast, MRI has excellent spatial resolution and provides a deep tissue penetration depth that can be used for 3-dimensional human body imaging. However, MRI suffers from low sensitivity, while FI has a high sensitivity even for a single cell. Therefore, a combination of FI and MRI techniques can result in accurate and reliable information for medical diagnosis. By providing detailed imaging data that can be obtained through multi-modal imaging, drug delivery processes can be monitored non-invasively [9]. Fluorescence/magnetic resonance multimodal imaging technology is beneficial in diagnosis in terms of sensitive functional imaging and providing high-resolution histological information [7].

Nanostructures with fluorescence and magnetic properties have been used as multimodal therapeutic agents [2]. Semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) are an emerging class of fluorescent materials with fluorescence emission dependent on their size and chemical composition. By conjugating semiconductor QDs with paramagnetic Gd (III) chelates novel fluorescence/magnetic resonance multimodal imaging can be provided. The cytotoxicity of semiconductor QDs can be decreased if appropriate coatings are applied to the QDs. However, these coatings such as silica encapsulation increase the particle size range and make the synthesis process more complicated [10]. Several types of semiconductor QDs have been utilized in cancer diagnosis and bioimaging. Gd-doped QDs are attractive due to the size-dependent emission, photostability, narrow emission and broad absorption spectra, and strong fluorescence brightness. The heavy metal composition of classic semiconductor QDs such as CdTe, CdS, and CdSe leads to toxicity in the human body since they could release free radicals of heavy metals from the QD core into the bloodstream [9].

Dextran consists of repeating glucose subunits that form a biodegradable neutral bacterial exopolysaccharide. Dextran is soluble in organic solvents and water. Dextran can be blended with hydrophobic polymers or bioactive agents to form different structures [11]. This compound is one of the widely used drug carriers which is obtained from a biopolymer [12]. Furthermore, it has been used in different biomedical applications such as coating on the nanoparticles that are used in the human body [13] or composite with different nanostructures [14].

The incorporation of Gd into the solid matrices will result in the slowing of tumbling of the Gd complex which leads to an enhancement in r1 relaxivity [15]. Designing high contrast efficiency Gd-containing contrast agents with low toxicity, high stability, and a simple preparation procedure is desirable. CQDs that are doped with Gd3+ can be used for FI and MRI. In this research, CQDs were derived from dextran, as a biocompatible biopolymer. To our knowledge, the synthesis of CQD from dextran has not been reported before. The CQDs were doped with N and Gd3+ ions and were used for simultaneous FI and MRI.

Dextran and Polyethyleneimine (linear, MW 25000) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Gadovist® (gadobutrol), a prescription medicine called a gadolinium-based contrast agent, the source of the Gd ions, was provided by Bayer AG, Germany. Trypsin-EDTA, penicillin/streptomycin, glutaraldehyde, fetal bovine serum (FBS), and RPMI-1640 medium were obtained from GIBCO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Gd-doped carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs were synthesized through the hydrothermal method. Briefly, 0.5 ml of PEI was added to a solution of 25 ml deionized water and 25 ml ethanol (solution A). Then 0.18 g dextran, 2 ml of solution A, and 2 ml of Gadovist were added to 30 ml of deionized water and stirred for 30 min. The prepared solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C at different times (Fig. 1 ).

The functional groups and chemical bonding of the synthesized CQDs were studied with an FTIR spectrometer (Rayleigh WQF-520) in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. The UV–vis absorption of the samples was recorded on a Rayleigh-2601 UV–vis spectrometer. The PL emission of the samples by excitation in the range of 340–480 nm was measured by a Shimadzu-RF 6000 PL spectrometer. ICP-OES (Varian 730-ES) analysis was applied to determine the Gd content of the synthesized CQDs solutions. The zeta potential was recorded by an Anton Paar Litesizer 500 particle size analyzer. The morphology and crystallography of the CQDs were studied by FEI TECNAI F20 HRTEM. Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) studies of the CQDs were conducted with a Full Plus AFM. For the sample preparation, the CQDs solution was sonicated for 10 min and then, a drop of the solution was brought on a freshly cleaved mica surface and the solvent was evaporated at room temperature before inserting in the AFM.

The CQDs' ability for FI was studied by incubation of CQDs with fibroblasts (Mouse C34/connective tissue-L929) cells which were provided by the National Cell Bank of Iran, Pasteur Institute. The cells were cultured in an RPMI-1640 medium. Each ml of the medium contained 10 % FBS, 50 μg streptomycin, and 50 units of penicillin. The cells were cultured at 37 °C with 85 % humidity and 5 % CO2 in an incubator. The cells were detached by digestion with trypsin (0.25 %) for 1 min after 4 days of culture. The cells were prepared in suspensions with 4 × 104 cells/ml. Then, 500 μl of the cell suspension was added to the wells plate and incubated for 24 h. Subsequently, 3 % of the CQDs solution was added to the cells and incubated for 24 h. An inverted microscope (Nikon, TE-2000) was used to prepare fluorescence images.

To investigate the application of Gd-doped CQDs as MRI contrast agents, a 3 T S, MAGNETOM Prisma clinical MRI scanner was used. The relaxation times (T1 and T2) were recorded by the scanner for the samples with different concentrations of Gd-doped CQDs. T1-weighted MRI was recorded with a slice thickness of 2.0 mm, a field of view = 25 × 25 cm2, matrix size = 256 × 256, and repetition time/echo time (TR/TE) = 750/7.4 ms. Longitudinal relaxation rates with static TE of 7.4 ms and TR in the range of 100–10000 ms were measured.

Fig. 2 a shows the XRD pattern of the synthesized CQDs. The XRD pattern includes a peak at 2θ = 9.7 which is due to (001) of graphite [16]. The broad peak at 2θ = 25.4° is related to (002) planes of graphite and renders a d-spacing of 0.35 nm [17]. Fig. 2b represents the AFM images of the CQDs. The CQDs' heights on the mica surface are below 7.2 nm. The size distribution of the dots can be observed in Fig. 2c which confirms that the diameter of the dost is almost below 10 nm. The zeta potential distribution of the synthesized CQDs (Fig. 2d) reveals that the CQDs have a potential of 1.6 eV on their surfaces. The positive or negative potential on the surface of the dots could help the dots to disperse better.

Fig. 3 shows the FTIR spectra of the dextran, dextran that is hydrothermally treated, dextran and PEI solution which is hydrothermally treated for different times, as well as hydrothermally-treated dextran, PEI, and contrast agent solution. In the untreated dextran, the peak observed at 1635 cm−1 is due to water molecule bonding [18]. The peak at 1013 cm−1 is due to the chain flexibility present in dextran around the glycosidic bonds. The band at 991 cm−1 is assigned to the C–OH bending vibrations. The peaks at 1153 cm−1 and 1365 cm−1 can be attributed to the δ(C–O–H). The peak at 1268 cm−1 is due to the ν(C–O) stretching mode in the ring. The absorption bands between 1200 and 1500 cm−1 may be caused by CH deformation vibrations and δ(C–O–H) bending vibrations. The absorption band at 1432 cm−1 is due to CH2 group vibration. The bands at 2927 cm−1 and 3428 cm−1 can be related to ν(C–H) and ν(O–H) stretching vibrations [19]. In the FTIR spectra of the other two samples which have been synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of dextran and dextran + PEI, the absorption band seen in 1650 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration, indicating that the existence –CC− groups [20]. The peaks in 700–900 cm−1 are assigned to the aromatic out-of-plane C–H bending [21]. The peaks at 1461 cm−1 and 2931 cm−1 are due to the stretching and bending vibrations of –CH2- [22]. The peak observed at 1385 cm−1 corresponds to the O–H stretching [23]. The peak at 2852 cm−1 in the DEX-PEI-7h sample is due to the symmetric stretching bands of –CH2 groups. The peak at 1581 cm−1 represents C–NH groups [24]. The peaks in the range of 1319–1390 cm−1 can be assigned to the C–N stretching of the polysaccharide skeleton. The peak at 1391 cm−1 stems from O–H bending vibration [25]. The absorption in the range of 1250 cm−1 was attributed to C–O and C–N [26]. The absorption peak around 3400 cm−1 relates to O–H and N–H stretching vibration [27]. The peak at 1078 cm−1 can also be assigned to C–O [28]. In the sample that contains Gd3+ ions, some peak shifts could be observed compared to the samples without Gd3+. For example, the peak at 1078 cm−1 has shifted to 1083 cm−1 and the peak at 1592 cm−1 has shifted to 1596 cm−1.

The HRTEM images of the CQDs in Fig. 4 a show the dots have sizes below 10 nm. The fringes in Fig. 4b with an interplanar spacing of 0.24 nm are consistent with the (1120) lattice facet of graphene. This indicates that the CQDs consisted of crystalline parts composed of graphitic sp 2 carbon atoms [29].

Fig. 5 a is the UV–vis absorption spectra of the different hydrothermally synthesized samples. It can be seen that dextran which is hydrothermally heat treated (DEX-3h sample) has a weak absorption in a wide range without any distinct absorption peak. The hydrothermally treated sample of dextran + PEI shows an absorption peak at 280 nm which could be assigned to the n-π* transitions of the CO bond [30]. The absorption spectrum observed around 350 nm in the sample hydrothermally treated for 14 h (DEX-PEI-14h) and the sample with Gd ions (DEX-PEI-Gd-7h) is due to π-π* transitions [31]. Fig. 5b exhibits the fluorescence emission of the synthesized CQDs (DEX-PEI-Gd-7h sample) by excitation at wavelengths in the range of 340–480 nm. The emission spectra have maximum peaks in the range of 460–540 nm. It can be seen the peaks are shifted to higher wavelengths (red-shift) by increasing the excitation wavelength. The excitation wavelength-dependent PL emission is a characteristic of most CQDs [32]. As can be seen in Fig. 5c, the hydrothermally treated samples containing dextran and PEI have visible fluorescence emission under a UV lamp.

Fig. 6 is a comparison between the PL emission of the CQDs by excitation at different wavelengths. The samples of dextran and PEI that are hydrothermally treated for 3 h and 14 h have higher PL intensity by the excitation at a wavelength in the UV regions of the spectrum. However, when the CQDs are excited by wavelengths in the visible region of the spectrum, the sample that is hydrothermally treated for 7h has PL with higher intensity. Therefore, depending on the applied excitation wavelength, CQDs with desired PL emission spectra can be engineered.

Since the pH of the tumor microenvironment is lower than normal tissues, the effect of pH on the PL emission of the CQDs was investigated. Fig. 7 (a and b) demonstrate the effect of pH on the PL spectra of the CQDs in the pH range of 4–9 and Fig. 7(c and d) shows the maximum intensity of PL emission in different pH values by excitation at 340 nm and 380 nm, respectively. In acidic environments, the hydrogen bonds between surface oxygen-related groups of CQDs can self-assemble the CQDs into larger particles. Furthermore, in acidic conditions, the carbonyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of CQDs can be protonated [33]. By storing CQDs in acidic or alkaline conditions, slow reactions that occur in these conditions can enhance the PL of the CQDs [34]. By storing the CQDs in strong alkaline conditions, the carbonyl functional groups on the surface of the CQDs can be partly reduced to hydroxyl [35]. In contrast, under strongly acidic conditions and at room temperature, the oxygen-containing functional groups on the CQDs can be oxidized. These hydrogenation/deoxygenation and oxidation reactions can happen at a lower speed at milder alkaline and acidic conditions. The PL in CQDs originates from quantum effect and surface defect (or a mechanism is currently unknown). The oxygen-containing groups on the surface of the CQDs can act as surface states with the energy levels between π and π* states of the CC which could introduce absorption bands due to these transitions. The content of the oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the CQDs increases due to both processes of deoxygenation in alkaline conditions and oxidation in acidic conditions which results in enhancement of the electron transition efficiency within these functional groups. Therefore, the CQDs have almost similar PL behavior in acidic and alkaline conditions [34]. Consequently, the almost preserved fluorescence emission of the synthesized CQDs in different acidic and alkaline conditions can be explained. The fluorescence emission of the synthesized CQDs has a small decay in alkaline conditions compared to acidic conditions.

The concentration of salt in the human body environment is 0.3 M and the tumor microenvironment has an acidic pH of 4–6 [9]. Therefore, the stability of the CQDs’ fluorescence in such an environment is an important factor for pharmaceutical applications. The effect of the ionic strength of the environments on the PL emission of the CQDs was studied by measuring their PL emission in NaCl solutions with different salt concentrations. Fig. 8 demonstrates the effect of ionic strength on the PL spectra of the synthesized CQDs. The PL emission at different excitation wavelengths confirms that the CQDs have photostability in ionic environments far beyond the ionic strength in the human body microenvironments.

The EDS elemental map of the CQDs solution is shown in Fig. 9 . The weight percent and atomic percent of the elements detected in the CQDs sample are also tabulated in Table S1. The elemental map confirms the presence of Gd along with C and O. The presence of Gd can make the CQDs contrast agents in MRI. The map shows that the Gd distribution in the sample is almost uniform.

The in-vitro bioimaging of the synthesized CQDs was investigated by their incubation with connective tissue-L929 fibroblast cells of the mouse. The prepared fluorescence photographs under an inverted microscope can be seen in Fig. 10 . The CQDs have penetrated the cells and show fluorescence in the cells upon excitation. The cells showed to have successfully been painted by the CQDs. The CQDs' penetration into the cells has occurred via endocytosis. Therefore, due to the good fluorescence emission, stability in environments with different pH values, and various ionic strengths, the synthesized CQDs can be effective fluorescence probes for bioimaging.

The doped Gd3+ ions into the CQDs structure can render them the MRI modality since it can accelerate the longitudinal relaxation of water protons. The magnetic relaxation behavior of the synthesized CQDs was studied by preparing CQDs with different dilutions. The T1-weighted MR images can be seen in Fig. 11 a. The gray-scaled T1-weighted images of the samples show a positive contrast enhancement. The brightness of the samples in T1-weighted images becomes enhanced by increasing the concentration of Gd3+. The specific relaxivity of the sample (r1) was determined by the slope of the linear plot of longitudinal relaxation rates (1/T1) as a function of the Gd concentration in the samples. The synthesized CQDs have a longitudinal relaxivity value of 0.272 mM−1 s−1 (Fig. 11b). Since longitudinal relaxivity depends on the interactions between hydrogen protons and Gd3+ ions, using small-sized nanoparticles is beneficial [36]. The Gd-doped CQDs are advantageous in terms of having a high specific surface area for better interactions as MRI contrast agents.

The synthesized CQDs in this research are superior in terms of PL emission within a wide wavelength range, as well as PL emission in environments with different pH values and ionic strength. Table 1 compares the CQDs in this research with similar works on CQDs.

In this research, CQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of dextran as the carbon source, PEI as the N-doping agent, and Gadovist (a drug) as the Gd-doping agent. The synthesized CQDs showed excitation wavelength-dependent PL emission by varying excitation wavelength in the range of 340–380 nm. The CQDs preserved their PL emission in environments with different ionic strengths created with NaCl solution in the range of 0.005–0.5 M. The CQDs were also able to emit fluorescence in acidic and basic environments which extend their area of applications. The CQDs successfully penetrated mouse fibroblast cells and emitted PL. Due to doped Gd, the CQDs showed a bright contrast in the T1-weighted MRI. Therefore, the Gd-doped CQDs have the potential to be applied as probes for multimodal FI and MRI. The obtained CQDs can also be evaluated for application in drug delivery systems. Furthermore, in-vivo experiments can better determine the success of these nanostructures in biomedical applications.
The datasets used and/or analyzed during the current study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

Hanieh Ghahremani: Software, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Mohammad Jafar Molaei: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Project administration, Conceptualization. Esmaeil Salimi: Project administration, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author would like to thank the Shahrood University of Technology, the Iran National Brain Mapping Laboratory, and the Iran Nanotechnology Initiative Council for the financial support of this project.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Properties and application of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in biosensors for disease detection: a comprehensive review
J. Drug Deliv. Sci. Technol.

Sustainable Materials and Technologies

Acta Biomater.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile preparation of gadolinium (iii) chelates functionalized carbon quantum dot-based contrast agent for magnetic resonance/fluorescence multimodal imaging
